Northrop Grumman Asks FTC to Drop Orbital ATK Acquisition Consent Order
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall


Northrop Grumman petitioned the Federal Trade Commission to drop a 20-year consent order imposed on the company in connection with its acquisition of Ortibal ATK.

In 2018, the FTC issued an order that alleged that there was a duopoly in the market to supply solid rocket motors. It noted that the acquisition would make Northrop Grumman both an SRM supplier and a player in the missile system prime contract market. As a result, it imposed non-discrimination and information protection obligations in certain markets as a condition of clearing the $7.8 billion acquisition.

Since 2018, the SRM market has changed substantially, going from "a market with two major suppliers into one filled with new entrants and competitively funded by both government and private investment," Northrop Grumman said in its petition.

"Given these developments, setting aside the Order will not harm competition," the defense contractor said. "These changed conditions spurred by market participants overcoming barriers to entry and increased demand have eliminated any possible incentive Northrop Grumman may have had to discriminate in the provision of SRMs."

The company's petition was published by the FTC on Thursday, opening a 30-day public comment period.


Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

04-02-26 1730ET

data.title: Tech Advances on Hopes For AI Outlook - Tech Roundup
data.description:
Shares of technology companies rose as traders bet the artificial-intelligence boom will survive an energy shock.

Chip makers continued a recent recovery rally, led by formerly embattled Intel.

Chatbot maker OpenAI acquired TBPN, an online talk show that aims to compete with Bloomberg and CNBC in by-the-minute analysis of technology news and executive interviews. OpenAI declined to disclose the terms of the transaction, vowing to maintain its editorial independence.

Maine is poised to freeze large data-center construction, which would make it the first state to enact such a measure as communities across the U.S. grapple with AI infrastructure controversy.

Amazon.com said it would impose a temporary 3.5% fuel and logistics surcharge on third-party sellers as the company grapples with elevated fuel costs during the Iran war.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

04-02-26 1729ET

data.title: Consumer Cos Down as Energy Shock Weighs - Consumer Roundup
data.description:
Consumer companies fell as fears about an energy shock offset generally strong economic data.

The average price of a gallon of regular gasoline in the U.S. has risen to $4.08, according to AAA. That's likely to go higher after oil futures in New York topped $111 a barrel.

U.S. jobless claims declined unexpectedly in the latest weekly tally, falling 9,000 to 202,000, and showing little sign so far of damage from the energy shock.

Home-goods store Bed Bath & Beyond agreed to acquire storage and organization retailer The Container Store and its brands Elfa and Closet Works in a deal worth $150 million.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Health Care Down, Trimming Gains - Health Care Roundup
data.description:
Health-care companies fell, trimming recent gains, as traders rotated into sectors more sensitive to shifts in the geopolitical outlook.

The U.S. will impose tariffs of up to 100% on branded pharmaceuticals, the White House said Thursday, though nations or drugmakers that strike deals with the Trump administration or commit to build manufacturing facilities in the U.S. can receive lower levies.

Eli Lilly pared its gains for the week.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: Industrials Down on Energy-Shock Duration Fears - Industrials Roundup
data.description:
Shares of industrial and transportation companies ticked down amid concerns about the duration of an energy shock.

French carrier Air France-KLM and German airline Deutsche Lufthansa said they had submitted nonbinding offers for a minority stake in Portugal's state-owned airline TAP Air Portugal.

Federal regulators are weighing a ban on air-bag components from an obscure Chinese supplier after finding the parts were to blame for 10 deaths in a dozen crashes over the past three years.

data.title: SpaceX Eyes IPO Valuation of Over $2 Trillion
Materials Down With Gold Futures - Materials Roundup
data.description:
Producers of metals, grains and other raw materials ticked down alongside precious-metals futures.

The war-related energy shock pushed up inflation and the U.S. dollar, weighing on precious metals.

On Wednesday evening, the shock returned with a vengeance after President Trump failed to provide any timeline for an end to the war.

Gold futures fell 2.8% to $4651.50 a troy ounce, trimming weekly gains to 3.5%.

Even as energy markets priced in a prolonged, traders in sensitive sectors such as materials, responded to reports that Oman and the United Kingdom, among others were seeking to reopen the Strait of Hormuz.

"Many market participants appear to be positioning for a bullish Iran-resolution scenario," said Lorenzo Di Mattia, manager of hedge fund Sibilla Global Fund. "Even a modest delay in any meaningful resolution could create significant upside risk in oil, helium and related markets."


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

04-02-26 1729ET

Energy Up With Oil Futures - Energy Roundup
data.description:
Shares of energy companies rose alongside oil futures after President Trump's address to the nation spurred expectations that the Strait of Hormuz will be closed for a prolonged period.

The price of West Texas Intermediate futures, the U.S. benchmark, rose 11% to $111.54 a barrel, bringing its year-to-date gains to roughly 94%.

Strategists at brokerage Jefferies said a clean resolution to the Iran war was highly unlikely in the short term.

"Military escalation is feasible but unlikely to be decisive," said the Jefferies strategists, in a note to clients. "Diplomatic activity would suggest momentum toward a minimal agreement," the strategists added.

The strikes President Trump has threatened on Iranian energy facilities would cause escalation, and a regional conflict is likely to continue even after the U.S. concludes its operations, the Jefferies strategists warned.


Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

Great-grandson carries on ancestor's birthright citizenship fight
data.description:
STORY: :: A birthright citizenship pioneer's descendant protests Trump's bid to limit the constitutional right

:: Norman Wong, Great-grandson of Wong Kim Ark

"I think this is a special day for me. I see these people and I feel like I definitely don't stand alone, that if I can help empower them, great. Because in the end, it's going to take America as a whole to stand up and to make this country right, to keep this ship balanced."

:: His great-grandfather Wong Kim Ark was declared a non-citizen by customs officials despite being born in the U.S.

:: Washington, D.C. / April 1, 2026

:: Wong Kim Ark went to the Supreme Court in 1898 and won, but was still subjected to harsh treatment

WONG: "Over 125 years ago, Wong Kim Ark fought for his rightful place as an American. In 1898 he won his case before the United States Supreme Court, affirming the 14th Amendment that guarantees citizenship to those born on U.S. soil. // Wong Kim Ark was working, from what I understand, for the railroad, along the border. // And on one of his comings and goings on the border they decided to detain him, somewhere around 1900, so that was two years or so after the decision in 1898. // The customs officials knew who he was, and yet they still jailed him and he had his papers. He didn't have to have papers, they didn't make whites carry papers, but they made him carry papers. They still jailed him. So what does that say? That he had second-class rights even though he was born American."

Over a century ago, his great-grandfather was told he was not a citizen despite being born in the United States. Wong Kim Ark went to the Supreme Court and won.

Wong Kim Ark's victory in court was not enough, despite having secured his citizenship he was subjected to harsh treatment as he worked as a railroad worker along the U.S./Mexico border, questioned when he would try to re-enter the country.

More than one hundred years later, Norman Wong made the trip from San Francisco to Washington, standing on Wednesday (April 1) outside the same court his great-grandfather once stood before.

Wong does not believe the Supreme Court will rule entirely in favor of President Trump's executive order, but he expects them to begin placing limits on birthright citizenship.

Trump fires Pam Bondi as US attorney general
data.description:
STORY: President Donald Trump on Thursday removed Pam Bondi from her post as U.S. Attorney General.

The move follows mounting frustration with her performance, including her handling of files related to Jeffrey Epstein, which drew renewed scrutiny of Trump's past friendship with the late financier and sex offender.

:: February 11, 2026

BONDI: 'You don't tell me anything, you washed up loser lawyer..you're not even a lawyer."

During a combative hearing before a House of Representatives panel earlier this year, Bondi responded to criticism with political attacks directed at lawmakers. She also refused to apologize or look at Epstein victims and their relatives who attended the proceedings.

:: February 11, 2026

BONDI: "So he asked a four minute question. and I am going to answer."

Trump had also reportedly grown frustrated that Bondi was not moving quickly enough to prosecute his critics and adversaries.

:: September 26, 2025

TRUMP: "These were corrupt, radical left Democrats, because Comey essentially was a--he's worse than a Democrat."

The targets included former FBI Director James Comey and New York Attorney General Letitia James, neither of whom faced criminal charges after investigations, with both cases ultimately dismissed.

Bondi's ouster could lead to a shake-up in strategy at the Justice Department and potentially a renewed push to deploy the U.S. legal system against Trump's enemies.

In the interim, Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche, Trump's former personal lawyer, will lead the Justice Department.

Appeals panel orders resentencing of ex-Colorado clerk over voting machines
data.description:
April 2 (Reuters) - A Colorado appeals court on Thursday overturned the nine-year prison sentence, but not the conviction, given to Tina Peters, a former county clerk convicted of tampering with voting machines in her pursuit of claims the 2020 presidential election had been stolen from Donald Trump, as Trump repeatedly and falsely claimed.

Trump has taken up the case of Peters, a fellow Republican, in recent months and put pressure on state courts and Colorado Governor Jared Polis, a Democrat, to free her, saying she is a political prisoner. Polis last month signaled he may be willing to consider clemency for Peters.

A three-judge appeals court panel ordered a lower court to resentence Peters, who remains jailed. The panel upheld her conviction on all seven counts, which include attempts to influence a public servant and violation of duty, among others. 

In its ruling, the panel said the lower court judge Matthew Barrett improperly punished Peters based on her protected speech - her claims of election fraud, which she maintained throughout her 2024 trial. The panel cited several things Barrett said at trial, including calling Peters a "charlatan" who continued to peddle "snake oil that's been proven to be junk time and time again."

"The trial court's comments about Peters's belief in the existence of 2020 election fraud went beyond relevant considerations for her sentencing. Her offense was not her belief, however misguided the trial court deemed it to be, in the existence of such election fraud; it was her deceitful actions in her attempt to gather evidence of such fraud," the panel wrote.

The panel gave no suggestion of what Peters' sentence should be.

Peters was indicted in 2022 following an election security breach at her office that led to voting equipment passwords being posted on a right-wing blog. She denied wrongdoing.

Two attorneys for Peters, Peter Ticktin and John Case, did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

Trump pardoned Peters in December in a move that was considered symbolic since Peters is not in federal custody. The Colorado appeals court panel, in its ruling, wrote that a president's federal pardon powers do not reach state offenses. 

(Reporting by Brad Brooks in Colorado; editing by Donna Bryson and Chris Reese)

By Brad Brooks

data.title: Pakistan hikes fuel prices sharply amid spiralling Mideast conflict
data.description:
(Removes incorrect price hike percentage in headline and paragraph 1; corrects percentage increases for petrol and diesel in paragraph 2)

April 2 (Reuters) - Pakistan on Thursday hiked consumer prices for diesel and petrol sharply, its second increase in less than a month, amid rising global oil prices spurred by the conflict in the Middle East.

The price of diesel would be raised by 54.9% to 520.35 rupees ($1.88) per litre, and petrol by 42.7% to 458.40 rupees per litre.

"It was inevitable to raise the prices due to the international market prices going out of control after the US-Iran war," Pakistan's petroleum minister, Ali Pervaiz Malik, said in a news conference telecast on state television, along with the country's finance minister.

Last month, the South Asian nation raised consumer prices for diesel and petrol by about 20%, citing higher oil prices driven by the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.

The decision is likely to ripple through to higher inflation and hit Pakistan's impoverished population. Pakistan imports oil mainly from Saudi Arabia and the UAE through the Strait of Hormuz.

The country's finance minister Muhammad Aurangzeb separately in the news conference announced subsidies aimed at offering relief to small farmers, motorcyclists and intercity transportation of goods and passengers.

Malik said the government had given a subsidy of 129 billion rupees in the last three weeks, but it was no longer affordable due to the hike in international oil prices.

"Since the resources are limited and there is no end to this war in sight, there was no way to continue with a blanket subsidy," he said.

U.S. oil prices jumped more than 11% on Thursday, while Brent soared over 7% in volatile trading a day after U.S. President Donald Trump said military operations would be intensified.

($1 = 278.7000 Pakistani rupees) (Reporting by Asif Shahzad in Islamabad; Writing by Kanjyik Ghosh in Barcelona; Editing by Chris Reese)
